title: Neurofibromatosis Type 1 & 2
oneLiner: Autosomal dominant tumour predisposition syndromes; NF1 (von Recklinghausen) common, NF2 rare; both cause benign/malignant nerve sheath tumours, skin findings, and require lifelong surveillance for serious complications.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal cord compression** (NF1 ± NF2) – from rapid-growing plexiform neurofibroma/vestibular schwannoma. **Finding:** acute back/neck pain + limb weakness + urinary retention (sensory level). Must check urgent MRI spine before symptom irreversibility.
- **Hypertensive crisis from phaeochromocytoma** (NF1, ~2% risk). **Finding:** paroxysmal severe headache + palpitations + diaphoresis + labile BP. Measure BP lying/standing; do not give beta-blocker alone (unopposed alpha agonism). Confirm with 24h urinary metanephrines or plasma metanephrines.
- **Raised ICP from obstructive hydrocephalus** (NF2, bilateral acoustic neuromas). **Finding:** progressive headache, vomiting, gait ataxia, papilloedema, declining GCS. Urgent CT head + neurosurgery review.
- **Malignant transformation to MPNST** (NF1, lifetime risk ~8-15%). **Red flag:** rapid expansion of a pre-existing neurofibroma, new pain, new neurological deficit, >5 cm size. Do not dismiss as “another lump” — urgent MRI + biopsy.
sections.Presentation:
- **NF1:**
  - Café-au-lait spots (>6, >5 mm pre-pubertal; >15 mm post-pubertal)
  - Axillary/inguinal freckling
  - Cutaneous/subcutaneous neurofibromas (often not painful)
  - Plexiform neurofibromas (deep, may cause disfigurement/compression)
  - Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas, seen on slit lamp)
  - Optic pathway glioma (can cause visual loss/proptosis)
  - Skeletal dysplasia (scoliosis, tibial pseudarthrosis)
  - Learning difficulties, ADHD (common)
  - Hypertension (essential or renovascular from renal artery stenosis)
- **NF2:**
  - Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (hearing loss, tinnitus, imbalance)
  - Other intracranial/spinal schwannomas and meningiomas
  - Juvenile cataracts (subcapsular, often bilateral)
  - Skin tumours (plaque-like, less than NF1)
  - Peripheral nerve schwannomas (weakness, paraesthesia)
  - Fewer café-au-lait spots (usually <6)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **NF1 vs NF2:** Splitter — **presence of bilateral acoustic neuromas on MRI** is diagnostic of NF2. Also NF2 lacks axillary freckling and Lisch nodules.
- **Other phakomatoses:**
  - **Tuberous sclerosis:** hypomelanotic macules (ash-leaf), facial angiofibromas, seizures, no neurofibromas.
  - **McCune-Albright syndrome:** café-au-lait spots with irregular borders (coast of Maine), polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, precocious puberty; no neurofibromas.
  - **Legius syndrome:** café-au-lait spots and freckling, but no neurofibromas or Lisch nodules; SPRED1 mutation.
- **Isolated cafe-au-lait spots:** common in normal population; need >6 to meet NF1 criteria.
- **Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour:** differentiate from benign neurofibroma — rapid growth, pain, MRI heterogeneity, PET avidity.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - BP measurement (lying/standing) – screen for hypertension/phaeochromocytoma
  - Fundoscopy – papilloedema, optic atrophy, Lisch nodules (ophthalmology slit lamp)
  - Neurological exam (cranial nerves, limb power, sensory level)
  - Audiometry (if NF2 suspected)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting (baseline)
  - Plasma/24h urinary metanephrines (if hypertension or phaeochromocytoma suspected)
  - Genetic testing: NF1 (NF1 gene), NF2 (NF2 gene, Merlin protein) – refer to clinical genetics
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain + whole spine (with gadolinium) for all suspected NF1/NF2 at diagnosis; annual screening for optic glioma/vestibular schwannoma in children
  - **Second-line:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if suspecting MPNST or phaeochromocytoma; PET-CT for malignant lesions
  - **Eye:** slit-lamp exam for Lisch nodules (NF1) and cataracts (NF2); retinal exam for retinal hamartomas (NF2)
- **Special tests:**
  - Nerve conduction studies/EMG if peripheral neuropathy
  - Biopsy of suspicious lesions (for histology + grading)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **NF1 (NIH Consensus 1987):** ≥2 of: 6+ café-au-lait spots; 2+ neurofibromas/1 plexiform; axillary/inguinal freckling; optic glioma; 2+ Lisch nodules; distinctive bone lesion (sphenoid wing dysplasia, tibial pseudarthrosis); first-degree relative with NF1.
  - **NF2 (Manchester criteria):** bilateral vestibular schwannomas; or unilateral VS + first-degree relative with NF2; or two of: meningioma, glioma, schwannoma, juvenile cataract, cerebral calcification.
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI brain with contrast** – omission can miss acoustic neuroma, optic glioma, or meningioma causing compressive symptoms. Also **BP and 24h urinary metanephrines before surgery** – undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma can cause fatal hypertensive crisis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected spinal cord compression: high-dose dexamethasone (10 mg IV stat) + urgent MRI spine + neurosurgery referral.
  - If suspected phaeochromocytoma: do not stimulate (avoid palpation, stress). Start phenoxybenzamine 10 mg BD PO (alpha-blockade) + propranolol (after alpha-blockade) – [check local endocrine guidelines]. Admit to HDU/ICU.
  - If raised ICP: IV dexamethasone (8-16 mg), neurosurgery review, discuss emergency VP shunt.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Multidisciplinary input: neurology, neurosurgery, ophthalmology, genetics, pain team.
  - Pain management: neuropathic pain (gabapentin, amitriptyline). Avoid opioids unless severe.
  - Wound care if surgical excision planned.
  - Consider psychological support (body image, learning difficulties).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Annual surveillance (age-specific): BP, ophthalmology, MRI brain (NF2 more frequent), hearing tests (audiology).
  - Genetic counselling (autosomal dominant, 50% risk to offspring).
  - Surgery for symptomatic tumours (schwannoma excision, neurofibroma debulking).
  - Stereotactic radiosurgery for small vestibular schwannomas (NF2).
  - Chemotherapy (e.g., MEK inhibitors for plexiform neurofibroma in NF1 – specialist only).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Not typically required unless for specific surgery or complication. If post-op: NBM until neurosurgery review, IV fluids (Hartmann’s 1 ml/kg/h), VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (unless spinal surgery – discuss with team). Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Phaeochromocytoma (pre-op):**
  - Phenoxybenzamine: 10 mg PO BD, titrate to BP (max 1 mg/kg/day). Alpha-blocker only; never give beta-blocker first.
  - Propranolol: 40 mg PO TDS (start 3 days after alpha-blockade). [Individualise based on heart rate; refer to local guidelines.]
- **Neuropathic pain:**
  - Gabapentin: start 300 mg nocte, titrate to pain (max 3600 mg daily in divided doses). Reduce dose in renal impairment.
  - Amitriptyline: 10-25 mg nocte, max 150 mg.
- **Corticosteroids:**
  - Dexamethasone: acute cord compression – 10 mg IV stat, then 4-8 mg IV/PO BD, taper.
- **Chemotherapy:**
  - MEK inhibitors (e.g., selumetinib) for inoperable plexiform neurofibroma – specialist oncology only.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Beta-blockers in phaeochromocytoma without prior alpha-blockade → hypertensive crisis.
  - Gabapentin/amitriptyline: avoid abrupt withdrawal – taper.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if CrCl <60 ml/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Phenoxybenzamine given orally in an undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma crisis without BP monitoring** – can cause profound hypotension if not titrated.
  - **Steroid overuse (especially dexamethasone) without glucose monitoring** – can precipitate DKA in undiagnosed diabetics.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Spinal cord compression / cauda equina syndrome
  - Phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis
  - Obstructive hydrocephalus (NF2)
  - Malignant transformation (MPNST)
  - Visual loss from optic glioma
  - Haemorrhage from tumour (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hearing loss / tinnitus (NF2)
  - Scoliosis, leg length discrepancy, pseudarthrosis (NF1)
  - Learning difficulties, ADHD, autism (NF1)
  - Disfigurement from neurofibromas
  - Secondary malignancy (especially MPNST, glioblastoma)
  - Cardiovascular disease from hypertension/renovascular disease
sections.Prognosis:
- **NF1:** Normal life expectancy in mild cases, but reduced by ~10-15 years due to malignancy, hypertension, and vascular complications. Optic glioma often indolent; MPNST is aggressive.
- **NF2:** Morbidity high due to hearing loss and multiple tumours. Median survival ~50-60 years; death from brainstem compression or malignant transformation.
- Both require lifelong surveillance.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check BP and 24h urinary metanephrines before any surgery in NF1** – phaeochromocytoma is a common cause of perioperative death.
- **Do not dismiss a new or growing lump in NF1** – refer urgently for MRI ± biopsy to rule out MPNST.
- **Refer to clinical genetics** for all confirmed cases – important for family screening and prenatal counselling.
- **Screen children annually for optic glioma** (ophthalmology + MRI brain) up to age 8.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use beta-blockers alone in hypertensive crisis** – unopposed alpha agonism leads to severe hypertension.
- **Do not ignore new peripheral neurological symptoms** – assume they are from a benign neurofibroma; often there is an underlying schwannoma or spinal cord compression.
- **Do not give steroids for presumed optic glioma without ophthalmology review** – may worsen prognosis in some cases (pilocytic astrocytoma).
- **Do not forget to ask about family history** – NF is autosomal dominant, missing this can lead to missed diagnosis in children.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** sudden onset of severe headache with vomiting (raised ICP), new limb weakness or bladder/bowel dysfunction (cord compression), severe abdominal/back pain with a new lump (MPNST), or uncontrollable hypertension with palpitations.
- **Follow-up:** specialist NF clinic (neurosurgery/neurology + clinical genetics). Frequency depends on age and subtype – annual for NF1, 6-monthly for NF2.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neurosurgery if:** new focal neurological deficit (especially acute onset), declining GCS, signs of cord compression (sensory level, urinary retention), or new onset seizures.
- **Call ICU if:** hypertensive crisis refractory to alpha-blockers (phaeochromocytoma), status epilepticus, or acute hydrocephalus with impending herniation.
- **Thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS <13, systolic BP <90 or >180 mmHg with symptoms, or any rapid deterioration.

rawMarkdown:
## Neurofibromatosis Type 1 & 2

**One-liner:** Autosomal dominant tumour predisposition syndromes; NF1 (von Recklinghausen) common, NF2 rare; both cause benign/malignant nerve sheath tumours, skin findings, and require lifelong surveillance for serious complications.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal cord compression** (NF1 ± NF2) – from rapid-growing plexiform neurofibroma/vestibular schwannoma. **Finding:** acute back/neck pain + limb weakness + urinary retention (sensory level). Must check urgent MRI spine before symptom irreversibility.
- **Hypertensive crisis from phaeochromocytoma** (NF1, ~2% risk). **Finding:** paroxysmal severe headache + palpitations + diaphoresis + labile BP. Measure BP lying/standing; do not give beta-blocker alone (unopposed alpha agonism). Confirm with 24h urinary metanephrines or plasma metanephrines.
- **Raised ICP from obstructive hydrocephalus** (NF2, bilateral acoustic neuromas). **Finding:** progressive headache, vomiting, gait ataxia, papilloedema, declining GCS. Urgent CT head + neurosurgery review.
- **Malignant transformation to MPNST** (NF1, lifetime risk ~8-15%). **Red flag:** rapid expansion of a pre-existing neurofibroma, new pain, new neurological deficit, >5 cm size. Do not dismiss as “another lump” — urgent MRI + biopsy.

### Presentation
- **NF1:**
  - Café-au-lait spots (>6, >5 mm pre-pubertal; >15 mm post-pubertal)
  - Axillary/inguinal freckling
  - Cutaneous/subcutaneous neurofibromas (often not painful)
  - Plexiform neurofibromas (deep, may cause disfigurement/compression)
  - Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas, seen on slit lamp)
  - Optic pathway glioma (can cause visual loss/proptosis)
  - Skeletal dysplasia (scoliosis, tibial pseudarthrosis)
  - Learning difficulties, ADHD (common)
  - Hypertension (essential or renovascular from renal artery stenosis)
- **NF2:**
  - Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (hearing loss, tinnitus, imbalance)
  - Other intracranial/spinal schwannomas and meningiomas
  - Juvenile cataracts (subcapsular, often bilateral)
  - Skin tumours (plaque-like, less than NF1)
  - Peripheral nerve schwannomas (weakness, paraesthesia)
  - Fewer café-au-lait spots (usually <6)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **NF1 vs NF2:** Splitter — **presence of bilateral acoustic neuromas on MRI** is diagnostic of NF2. Also NF2 lacks axillary freckling and Lisch nodules.
- **Other phakomatoses:**
  - **Tuberous sclerosis:** hypomelanotic macules (ash-leaf), facial angiofibromas, seizures, no neurofibromas.
  - **McCune-Albright syndrome:** café-au-lait spots with irregular borders (coast of Maine), polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, precocious puberty; no neurofibromas.
  - **Legius syndrome:** café-au-lait spots and freckling, but no neurofibromas or Lisch nodules; SPRED1 mutation.
- **Isolated cafe-au-lait spots:** common in normal population; need >6 to meet NF1 criteria.
- **Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour:** differentiate from benign neurofibroma — rapid growth, pain, MRI heterogeneity, PET avidity.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - BP measurement (lying/standing) – screen for hypertension/phaeochromocytoma
  - Fundoscopy – papilloedema, optic atrophy, Lisch nodules (ophthalmology slit lamp)
  - Neurological exam (cranial nerves, limb power, sensory level)
  - Audiometry (if NF2 suspected)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting (baseline)
  - Plasma/24h urinary metanephrines (if hypertension or phaeochromocytoma suspected)
  - Genetic testing: NF1 (NF1 gene), NF2 (NF2 gene, Merlin protein) – refer to clinical genetics
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain + whole spine (with gadolinium) for all suspected NF1/NF2 at diagnosis; annual screening for optic glioma/vestibular schwannoma in children
  - **Second-line:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if suspecting MPNST or phaeochromocytoma; PET-CT for malignant lesions
  - **Eye:** slit-lamp exam for Lisch nodules (NF1) and cataracts (NF2); retinal exam for retinal hamartomas (NF2)
- **Special tests:**
  - Nerve conduction studies/EMG if peripheral neuropathy
  - Biopsy of suspicious lesions (for histology + grading)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **NF1 (NIH Consensus 1987):** ≥2 of: 6+ café-au-lait spots; 2+ neurofibromas/1 plexiform; axillary/inguinal freckling; optic glioma; 2+ Lisch nodules; distinctive bone lesion (sphenoid wing dysplasia, tibial pseudarthrosis); first-degree relative with NF1.
  - **NF2 (Manchester criteria):** bilateral vestibular schwannomas; or unilateral VS + first-degree relative with NF2; or two of: meningioma, glioma, schwannoma, juvenile cataract, cerebral calcification.
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI brain with contrast** – omission can miss acoustic neuroma, optic glioma, or meningioma causing compressive symptoms. Also **BP and 24h urinary metanephrines before surgery** – undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma can cause fatal hypertensive crisis.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected spinal cord compression: high-dose dexamethasone (10 mg IV stat) + urgent MRI spine + neurosurgery referral.
  - If suspected phaeochromocytoma: do not stimulate (avoid palpation, stress). Start phenoxybenzamine 10 mg BD PO (alpha-blockade) + propranolol (after alpha-blockade) – [check local endocrine guidelines]. Admit to HDU/ICU.
  - If raised ICP: IV dexamethasone (8-16 mg), neurosurgery review, discuss emergency VP shunt.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Multidisciplinary input: neurology, neurosurgery, ophthalmology, genetics, pain team.
  - Pain management: neuropathic pain (gabapentin, amitriptyline). Avoid opioids unless severe.
  - Wound care if surgical excision planned.
  - Consider psychological support (body image, learning difficulties).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Annual surveillance (age-specific): BP, ophthalmology, MRI brain (NF2 more frequent), hearing tests (audiology).
  - Genetic counselling (autosomal dominant, 50% risk to offspring).
  - Surgery for symptomatic tumours (schwannoma excision, neurofibroma debulking).
  - Stereotactic radiosurgery for small vestibular schwannomas (NF2).
  - Chemotherapy (e.g., MEK inhibitors for plexiform neurofibroma in NF1 – specialist only).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Not typically required unless for specific surgery or complication. If post-op: NBM until neurosurgery review, IV fluids (Hartmann’s 1 ml/kg/h), VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (unless spinal surgery – discuss with team). Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2.

### Drugs & Doses

- **Phaeochromocytoma (pre-op):**
  - Phenoxybenzamine: 10 mg PO BD, titrate to BP (max 1 mg/kg/day). Alpha-blocker only; never give beta-blocker first.
  - Propranolol: 40 mg PO TDS (start 3 days after alpha-blockade). [Individualise based on heart rate; refer to local guidelines.]
- **Neuropathic pain:**
  - Gabapentin: start 300 mg nocte, titrate to pain (max 3600 mg daily in divided doses). Reduce dose in renal impairment.
  - Amitriptyline: 10-25 mg nocte, max 150 mg.
- **Corticosteroids:**
  - Dexamethasone: acute cord compression – 10 mg IV stat, then 4-8 mg IV/PO BD, taper.
- **Chemotherapy:**
  - MEK inhibitors (e.g., selumetinib) for inoperable plexiform neurofibroma – specialist oncology only.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Beta-blockers in phaeochromocytoma without prior alpha-blockade → hypertensive crisis.
  - Gabapentin/amitriptyline: avoid abrupt withdrawal – taper.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if CrCl <60 ml/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Phenoxybenzamine given orally in an undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma crisis without BP monitoring** – can cause profound hypotension if not titrated.
  - **Steroid overuse (especially dexamethasone) without glucose monitoring** – can precipitate DKA in undiagnosed diabetics.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Spinal cord compression / cauda equina syndrome
  - Phaeochromocytoma hypertensive crisis
  - Obstructive hydrocephalus (NF2)
  - Malignant transformation (MPNST)
  - Visual loss from optic glioma
  - Haemorrhage from tumour (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Hearing loss / tinnitus (NF2)
  - Scoliosis, leg length discrepancy, pseudarthrosis (NF1)
  - Learning difficulties, ADHD, autism (NF1)
  - Disfigurement from neurofibromas
  - Secondary malignancy (especially MPNST, glioblastoma)
  - Cardiovascular disease from hypertension/renovascular disease

### Prognosis
- **NF1:** Normal life expectancy in mild cases, but reduced by ~10-15 years due to malignancy, hypertension, and vascular complications. Optic glioma often indolent; MPNST is aggressive.
- **NF2:** Morbidity high due to hearing loss and multiple tumours. Median survival ~50-60 years; death from brainstem compression or malignant transformation.
- Both require lifelong surveillance.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check BP and 24h urinary metanephrines before any surgery in NF1** – phaeochromocytoma is a common cause of perioperative death.
- **Do not dismiss a new or growing lump in NF1** – refer urgently for MRI ± biopsy to rule out MPNST.
- **Refer to clinical genetics** for all confirmed cases – important for family screening and prenatal counselling.
- **Screen children annually for optic glioma** (ophthalmology + MRI brain) up to age 8.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use beta-blockers alone in hypertensive crisis** – unopposed alpha agonism leads to severe hypertension.
- **Do not ignore new peripheral neurological symptoms** – assume they are from a benign neurofibroma; often there is an underlying schwannoma or spinal cord compression.
- **Do not give steroids for presumed optic glioma without ophthalmology review** – may worsen prognosis in some cases (pilocytic astrocytoma).
- **Do not forget to ask about family history** – NF is autosomal dominant, missing this can lead to missed diagnosis in children.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** sudden onset of severe headache with vomiting (raised ICP), new limb weakness or bladder/bowel dysfunction (cord compression), severe abdominal/back pain with a new lump (MPNST), or uncontrollable hypertension with palpitations.
- **Follow-up:** specialist NF clinic (neurosurgery/neurology + clinical genetics). Frequency depends on age and subtype – annual for NF1, 6-monthly for NF2.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neurosurgery if:** new focal neurological deficit (especially acute onset), declining GCS, signs of cord compression (sensory level, urinary retention), or new onset seizures.
- **Call ICU if:** hypertensive crisis refractory to alpha-blockers (phaeochromocytoma), status epilepticus, or acute hydrocephalus with impending herniation.
- **Thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS <13, systolic BP <90 or >180 mmHg with symptoms, or any rapid deterioration.
